comments.author: @Shiine1st
comments.text: Its easy to magnify Kigal's clinch of power and rightly so... but the truth is People need to also consider the easier path this country could be led to like so many other rebels takeover... looking into his traditional dispute practice and cows sharing i hope his local solution for local problem approach and wisdom to implement survives and builds on elevating it.... regardless democratic or not a leaders work i think and legitimacy should be judged by his work rather than compliance to western system of democracy- especially for an African.

replies.author: @Shiine1st
replies.text: ​@WhereonEarth4K I give so much credit to the Rueansan people,  but the matter of fact is comparing their role  under Kigami's party vs his predecessors... the same public behaved according to their leaders' shadow. So without downplaying their courage, resilience and determination to move forward in lighting speed of recovery from one of the worst human catastrophe... I command this dictatorial regime' ability to change the course of this nation. I applaud them and would deflect their critics about their clinch to power. I do not condone apppositions disappearance for example... and i don't deny their rights to also want lead their country, yet i wouldn't shay away to point that they are after their own self interest in doing it so. Maybe they would do better than Kigami, maybe they wouldn't... but their lack of the ability to forgone their desire to power, and their campaigns against their nation in exile says so much in my opinion what they willing to sacrifice for-  fight for their chance to leadership or fight against their self interest. This doesn't mean Kigami people are perfect nor does it mean they are the only ones that can lead this nation to more prosperous future... it means their improvement is certain,  and comparing to their neighbors the contrast is clear... its not justifiable to undermine that accomplishments if your ultimate goal is  to make it better. Unless one's goal is to sit on the throne by all means.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K

comments.author: @attilagacsalyi5039
comments.text: Why they pump the CO2 back to the lake?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The CO2 is reinjected at a precise deep layer so it stays dissolved under pressure — releasing it into the air would just create the same suffocation risk above the lake instead of below. The methane is what gets burned for electricity; the CO2 goes back where it can't hurt anyone.

comments.text:
Paul Kagame stands unmistakably among the most remarkable leaders of the 21st century. The breadth of his accomplishments and the transformative impact of his leadership are evident for all the world to behold. 

Very few leaders in modern history could have accomplished what Paul Kagame has achieved in Rwanda, particularly when one considers the state in which the nation found itself scarcely three decades ago. Indeed, there are scarcely any who could have presided over such a profound national transformation.

comments.author: @cathyprice6959
comments.text: To whomever produce this amazing video, thank you. The story took a delicate history and balanced it against its current one. As a Western black American, I am so honored to witness the rise of this beautiful country and people who have built something wonderful. Keep going. Don't let western attitudes or opinions shape how you govern yourself. Learn the lessons. You are a jewel.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That connection across distance — what Rwanda's story means to people far from it — is something the film quietly hoped for. Thank you for sharing that. 🙏

comments.author: @cyusaernest5931
comments.text:
We are Rwandans.
We are the last warning of how deeply a country can be destroyed — but also the strongest example of how quickly a nation can rise again.
From pain, we chose unity. From ashes, we built hope.
Our journey proves that with vision, discipline, and the right leadership, a nation can rebuild itself stronger than ever before.
Leadership is everything.

comments.author: @ThePilgrimProgress
comments.text: Paul Kagame is inarguably the best president and leader in Africa, turning a genocide landscape around in just two decades, transforming a disaster-laced country into a first world nation on a continent riddled with countries independent from colonialism for over six decades still wallowing in abject depravity due to corrupt and inept visionless leaders. Kagame is a history by itself and as a keen Africa watcher, I can’t help but to deify such visionary leader who has even transformed the citizens of Rwanda into one tribal-less law abiding citizens who have mastered every atom of “rule of law” that other Afro countries lack and overlook.  South Africa, Nigeria, Ghana, Gambia, Sierra Leone, Uganda, Tanzania, Zimbabwe etc , all British empire nations are rotten to the core due to political corruption and it is a sad reflection of why a nation as south Africa is a pathetic example of why independence is sometimes not a good thing for some countries but the Sahelian AES model seems fantastic. Forever proud of Rwanda.
